steps.text: " The Chilean government officially declared the with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: The most widespread version of its origins relat
steps.text: The most widespread version of its origins relates it with the zamacueca which arose in Peru as a variation of Spanish Fandango dancing with criollo.

steps.action: The dance is then thought to have passed to Chil
steps.text: The dance is then thought to have passed to Chile and Bolivia, where its name was shortened and where it continued to evolve.

steps.action: Due to the dance's popularity in the region, the
steps.text: Due to the dance's popularity in the region, the Peruvian evolution of the zamacueca was nicknamed "la chilena", "the Chilean", due to similarities between the dances.

steps.action: The Marinera, Zamba and the Cueca styles are dis
steps.text: The Marinera, Zamba and the Cueca styles are distinct from each other and from their root dance, the zamacueca.

steps.action: Another theory is that Cueca originated in the e
steps.text: Another theory is that Cueca originated in the early nineteenth century bordellos of South America, as a pas de deux facilitating partner finding.

steps.action: The usual interpretation of this courting dance
steps.text: The usual interpretation of this courting dance is zoomorphic: it tries to reenact the courting ritual of a rooster and a hen.

steps.action: The dance often finishes with the man kneeling o
steps.text: The dance often finishes with the man kneeling on one knee, with the woman placing her foot triumphantly on his raised knee.

steps.action: The same could be said with the music where in d
steps.text: The same could be said with the music where in different regions rhythm and speed slightly differ amongst the regions.

steps.action: While dancing, handkerchiefs are used by both ma
steps.text: While dancing, handkerchiefs are used by both male and female dancers by twirling over the head.

steps.action: The Chilean cueca is not just Zamacueca; it is a
steps.text: The Chilean cueca is not just Zamacueca; it is a blend of different dances of the time, with Zamacueca being just one influential aspect.

steps.action: The Chilean cueca is highly structured in terms
steps.text: The Chilean cueca is highly structured in terms of dance, lyrics, and music, akin to early European dances from colonial times.

steps.action: To understand its evolution, one should observe
steps.text: To understand its evolution, one should observe the different Spanish, European, and popular dances of the era.

steps.action: The Arab-Andalusian influence is considered the
steps.text: The Arab-Andalusian influence is considered the most significant contribution to cueca in all aspects—dancing, singing, and tempo—and is regarded as its primary root.

steps.action: During the second half of the nineteenth century
steps.text: During the second half of the nineteenth century, cueca spread to various Latin American countries, where the dance became known simply as the "chilena" (Chilean).

steps.action: Unlike the northeast and central west, in Buenos
steps.text: Unlike the northeast and central west, in Buenos Aires the dance was known as "cueca" rather than "chilena", with documented presence from the 1850s onward.

steps.action: Similarly, in Bolivia, like much of Argentina, t
steps.text: Similarly, in Bolivia, like much of Argentina, the dance was referred to as "chilena".

steps.action: Chilean sailors and adventurers carried cueca to
steps.text: Chilean sailors and adventurers carried cueca to the Mexican coast, specifically in Guerrero and Oaxaca, where the dance was also called "chilena".

steps.action: In Peru, the dance became immensely popular duri
steps.text: In Peru, the dance became immensely popular during the 1860s and 1870s and was also known as the "chilena".

steps.action: Cueca was officially declared the national dance
steps.text: Cueca was officially declared the national dance of Chile due to its significant presence throughout the country's history, announced via a public decree in the Official Journal (Diario Oficial) on November 6, 1979.

steps.action: In this form of cueca, a woman (arpillerista) da
steps.text: In this form of cueca, a woman (arpillerista) dances alone while holding a photograph of her disappeared loved one.

steps.action: During the television campaign advertising the "
steps.text: During the television campaign advertising the "No" option in the 1988 Chilean national plebiscite, a group of women (played by real-life relatives of the disappeared) performed the cueca sola.

steps.action: The symbolic gesture of cueca sola inspired the
steps.text: The symbolic gesture of cueca sola inspired the 1987 song "They Dance Alone (Cueca Solo)" by Sting.

steps.action: The cueca dance itself symbolizes a rooster-chic
steps.text: The cueca dance itself symbolizes a rooster-chicken relationship.
